visual_notes: Morganite's soft rose-peach color is inherently gentle and warm — less assertive than ruby, less cold than pink sapphire. Large clean crystals are available. The rose-gold + morganite combination became dominant in contemporary jewelry design in the 2010s.
prompt_fragments: morganite: peach-rose beryl from Cs-Li pegmatite — warm manganese hexagonal form
Madagascar morganite tabular crystal — blush warmth in beryllium silicate
rose-gold + morganite: 2010s design archetype in soft pink beryl
vibe_notes: Morganite is the warm, quiet sister of emerald and aquamarine — the peachy pink end of beryl's spectrum. It lacks the drama of other beryls but has a consistent warmth and gentleness. The rose-gold era made it a design archetype.
folklore_summary: Morganite is a relatively recently named gem variety (1911) compared to emerald and aquamarine. George Frederick Kunz named it for J.P. Morgan, one of the great mineral collectors of the Gilded Age — whose collections are now at the American Museum of Natural History and Harvard's Mineralogical Muse
